assertions.text: Output contains a markdown table (using | column | syntax)
assertions.evidence: | Scenario | Age | Credit Score | Min Score (Policy) | Income Status | Approved? | Rejection Reason? |

assertions.text: At least one output column name ends with '?' (e.g. 'Eligible?' or 'Approved?')
Column names use domain/business language — no implementation terms like 'creditScore', 'isEligible', 'boolean', 'int'
assertions.evidence: Columns are: Scenario, Age, Credit Score, Min Score (Policy), Income Status, Approved?, Rejection Reason? — all plain business language

assertions.text: Table includes at least one row specifically for the senior applicant (65+) threshold scenario
assertions.evidence: Rows include 'At senior threshold' (age 65, score 600) and 'Just above senior threshold' (age 65, score 601)

assertions.text: The missing income scenario is represented — either as a row with an open/uncertain expected value (?, TBD, or blank) or as an explicit open question note
assertions.evidence: Rows 'Missing income, good score' and 'Senior, missing income, above threshold' both have '?' in the Approved? column, and point 3 explicitly calls this 'the key open question'

assertions.text: Scenario names describe conditions ('Senior applicant at threshold') not outcomes ('Approved')
assertions.evidence: Scenario names such as 'Just above standard threshold', 'Unstable income, good score', 'One year below senior age', 'Missing income, good score' all describe input conditions, not outcomes
